perturbation.original_text: 8.1.1 Governing Law. This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the internal laws of the State of Washington, and, where such laws are preempted by the laws of the United States, by the internal laws of the United States, in each case without regard to conflicts of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.1.1 Governing Law. This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of a jurisdiction chosen by Primus, without regard to conflicts of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the reference to Washington and US law and replacing it with a jurisdiction chosen by Primus introduces a legal risk. It potentially violates consumer protection laws that mandate the application of local laws to contracts involving local consumers.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts RCW 19.86.020 by allowing Primus to select a jurisdiction whose laws may not adequately protect consumers in Washington state, potentially leading to unfair or deceptive practices. This is because the choice of law clause could override the protections offered by the Washington Consumer Protection Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.86.020: Unfair competition, practices, declared unlawful. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.86 > Section 19.86.020 19.86.010 << 19.86.020 >> 19.86.023 PDF RCW 19.86.020 Unfair competition, practices, declared unlawful. Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. [ 1961 c 216 s 2 .] NOTES: Hearing instrument dispensing, advertising, etc. — Application: RCW 18.35.180 .

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts RCW 19.86.020. The original text ensures that Washington state law, including its consumer protections, would govern the agreement. The changed text allows Primus to choose a jurisdiction, potentially one with weaker consumer protections, thus circumventing Washington law. RCW 19.86.020 declares "Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful.". By allowing a choice of law that may not adequately protect consumers in Washington, the modified text introduces the risk of unfair or deceptive practices, directly contradicting the statute's intent. The omission of the guarantee of Washington law constitutes a failure to ensure those protections are in place, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall protect Owner's Confidential Information from disclosure to others using the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own most highly confidential information, but in no event less than a reasonable standard of care.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall protect Owner's Confidential Information from disclosure to others.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'reasonable standard of care' clause weakens the protection of Confidential Information. This omission introduces legal risk because 'reasonable care' is a legally recognized standard. Its removal creates ambiguity and could lead to disputes over what constitutes adequate protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the "reasonable standard of care" requirement, the modified clause contradicts the spirit and intent of the Washington Uniform Trade Secrets Act (RCW 19.108.010). This act requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets, and eliminating the 'reasonable standard of care' could be interpreted as a failure to take such necessary measures, thereby weakening the protection of trade secrets and potentially violating the act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.108.010: Definitions. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.108 > Section 19.108.010 Beginning of Chapter << 19.108.010 >> 19.108.020 PDF RCW 19.108.010 Definitions. Unless the context clearly requires otherwise, the definitions set forth in this section apply throughout this chapter. (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means; (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject

(3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, tru
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'reasonable standard of care' directly contradicts the intention of maintaining secrecy as required by RCW 19.108.010. Snippet 1 and Snippet 2 both define 'misappropriation' as the disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent, implying a duty to maintain secrecy. By removing the explicit 'reasonable standard of care', the changed text weakens the recipient's obligation to actively protect the information, potentially failing to meet the standard of care necessary to prevent misappropriation. This creates a legal loophole that could lead to disputes and weaken trade secret protection, creating a clear legal risk.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of any controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this Agreement or the breach or interpretation thereof, the parties shall, upon five days notice from either one to the other, submit themselves and the subject-matter of the dispute to arbitration in accordance with the Commercial Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association by a single, disinterested arbitrator appointed in accordance with such Rules.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of any controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this Agreement or the breach or interpretation thereof, the parties shall submit the subject-matter of the dispute to arbitration in accordance with rules decided by Primus.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the reference to the 'Commercial Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association' and replacing it with 'rules decided by Primus' allows Primus to dictate arbitration rules, potentially creating an unfair arbitration process. This introduces legal risk, violating principles of fair arbitration.
perturbation.law_explanation: The changed text contradicts the Federal Arbitration Act (9 U.S. Code § 10) by allowing Primus to potentially select biased or unfair arbitration rules. Section 10 outlines conditions under which an arbitration award can be vacated, including evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrator. Allowing Primus to decide the rules circumvents the established standards of fairness and impartiality expected in arbitration proceedings, potentially leading to a violation of the FAA if those rules result in a biased outcome.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) In any of the following cases the United States court in and for the district wherein the award was made may make an order vacating the award upon the application of any party to the arbitration— (1) where the award was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means; (2) where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, or either of them; (3) where the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct in refusing to postpone the hearing, upon sufficient cause shown, or in refusing to hear evidence pertinent and material to the controversy; or of any other misbehavior by which the rights of any party have been prejudiced; or (4) where the arbitrators exceeded their powers, or so imperfectly executed them that a mutual, final, and definite award upon the subject matter submitted was not made. (b) If an award is vacated and the time within which the agreement required the award to be made has not expired, the court may, in its discretion, direct a rehearing by the arbitrators. (c) The United States district court for the district wherein an award was made that was issued pursuant to section 580 of title 5 may make an order vacating the award upon the application of a person, other than a party to the arbitration, who is adversely affected or aggrieved by the award, if the use of arbitration or the award is clearly inconsistent with the factors set forth in section 572 of title 5 . (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 672 ; Pub. L. 101–552, § 5 , Nov. 15, 1990 , 104 Stat. 2745 ; Pub. L. 102–354, § 5(b)(4) , Aug. 26, 1992 , 106 Stat. 946 ; Pub. L. 107–169, § 1 , May 7, 2002 , 116 Stat. 132 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 10, 43 Stat. 885 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2002—Subsec. (a)(1) to
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the Federal Arbitration Act by allowing 'Primus' to dictate arbitration rules, which could lead to a biased or unfair arbitration process, conflicting with the impartiality expected in arbitration. 9 U.S. Code § 10 allows a court to vacate an arbitration award where there was 'evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators'. By removing the reference to the 'Commercial Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association' and instead deferring to rules decided by Primus, the contract opens the door to the selection of biased rules, potentially violating 9 U.S. Code § 10 if these rules result in a partial outcome.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall promptly notify Owner in writing prior to making any such disclosure, in order to facilitate Owner's efforts to protect its Confidential Information. In such circumstances, Recipient shall cooperate with
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient may notify Owner prior to making any such disclosure. Recipient may cooperate with
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall' to 'may' removes the mandatory requirement for the Recipient to notify the Owner before disclosing confidential information required by law or court order. This introduces legal risk because it hinders the Owner's ability to protect its confidential information as required by law.
perturbation.law_explanation: By making notification and cooperation optional, the modified text weakens the Owner's ability to seek protective orders as contemplated under the Washington Uniform Trade Secrets Act (RCW 19.108.030). This section allows for court orders to protect trade secrets, and removing the mandatory notification requirement impairs the Owner's opportunity to invoke these legal protections, potentially resulting in the misappropriation of trade secrets without adequate legal recourse.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.108.030: Remedies for misappropriation—Damages. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.108 > Section 19.108.030 19.108.020 << 19.108.030 >> 19.108.040 PDF RCW 19.108.030 Remedies for misappropriation — Damages. (1) In addition to or in lieu of injunctive relief, a complainant may recover damages for the actual loss caused by misappropriation. A complainant also may recover for the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing damages for actual loss. (2) If wilful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (1). [ 1981 c 286 s 3 .]

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the recipient to notify the owner before disclosing confidential information, whereas the modified version makes this notification optional. The Law Explanation suggests that mandatory notification is important for the owner to seek protective orders under the Washington Uniform Trade Secrets Act (RCW 19.108.030). Although the provided snippets from RCW 19.108.030 primarily address remedies for misappropriation and damages, the Law Explanation reasonably infers that reducing the owner's ability to protect trade secrets weakens their potential to claim damages or injunctive relief under this law, because they may not be able to prevent the misappropriation in the first place. By changing 'shall' to 'may', the modification directly impairs the owner's opportunity to invoke legal protections.
